content:
The frontline workforce barely shows up in the AI adoption data. I spent three months figuring out why, and what the gap actually looks like from the inside.

5 min read
Just now

--

Before I studied AI adoption, I ran a food manufacturing and retail business.
The back office kept getting easier to digitize. Forecasts, supplier comparisons, pricing, customer emails. All of it moved onto software, then onto better software, then onto AI.
The floor did not move at all. Packing, stocking, serving customers, catching a quality problem before it walked out the door. Still human, still physical, still real time.
When I later started reading the economics literature on how AI was entering the workforce, the adoption charts never seemed to include anyone I had worked a shift with. The people I had hired, trained, and worked next to for years were simply absent from the story the data was telling.
So I measured it.

The headline number
I took the Anthropic Economic Index, which observes millions of real Claude conversations, and joined it to BLS employment data and O*NET task categories across 585 occupations. The question was simple. Where does AI adoption actually stand in frontline service work, meaning the jobs that employ nearly a third of American workers?
The answer:
Frontline occupations hold 31.7% of US employment. They account for 11.1% of observed AI usage.
Software and math occupations are the mirror image. 3.4% of jobs. Roughly 37% of usage.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Per worker, retail sales work shows up in the data at about one-fortieth the rate of software work. Food service, about one-hundred-and-eightieth.
That is the gap. It is bigger than I expected, it is not closing, and the reasons it exists are not the ones I would have guessed.

Three things that surprised me
1. The gap is bigger than the official numbers suggest
The frontline category that looks most AI-engaged is administrative support. It turns out this is partly an illusion.
The government’s occupation codes still file several technical jobs, like Bioinformatics Technicians and Computer Operators, under historical clerical categories. These roles drag the average up.
Remove them, and the administrative support number nearly halves. It drops from 0.65 to 0.34.
The workers actually standing at registers and answering phones are further from the AI economy than the raw statistics say. When you look at the corrected numbers, the gap between frontline administrative work and back-office knowledge work widens, not narrows.
2. It is not a wage story, and it is not closing
I expected AI adoption to track pay. It does not.
Across all 585 occupations, wages explain almost none of the variance in usage. The gap exists because usage scales with occupations, not with workers. Retail salespersons, cashiers, and customer service reps each employ millions of people. Proportionate per-occupation adoption becomes disproportionate per-worker adoption.
This matters because the standard story about who benefits from new technology usually runs through wages. Higher-paid workers get access first, returns compound, gap widens. That story does not fit here. Something else is doing the work.
The gap is about people, not job titles.
And it is not narrowing. Comparing early- and late-2025 snapshots, every group’s usage grew roughly in place. Food service actually lost ground.
Adoption is deepening where it was already deep.
3. When AI reaches frontline work, it tends to replace rather than teach
The data classifies conversations by interaction style. Delegation (“do this for me”) versus collaboration (working through a task together).
Frontline administrative tasks show more delegation-style usage than software tasks do. 62.7% versus 58.1%.
That matters because recent experimental work from Anthropic finds that full delegation is precisely the interaction style associated with the weakest downstream skill formation. When a software engineer uses Claude, they are usually iterating with it, reading its output critically, learning something in the process. When a frontline administrative worker uses it, they are more often handing the task over completely and taking the answer.
The workers getting the least AI exposure are also getting the least learning value from the exposure they do get. This is a compounding disadvantage, not just a snapshot one.

A new divide is forming inside frontline work
The February 2026 data shows the gap reorganizing itself.
Customer Service Representatives now score among the most AI-exposed occupations in the entire economy, at 0.70. That is right next to Computer Programmers at 0.75. In two years, the job of taking customer complaints has moved from “barely touched by AI” to “heavily AI-mediated.”
Meanwhile, 39% of frontline occupations register literally zero. Every cook category. Every food prep worker. Home health aides. Janitors.
Within the same storefront, retail salespersons carry roughly four times the exposure of cashiers.
The dividing line is not prestige or pay. It is whether your work happens through a screen or in a room with another person.
This is a new axis of labor market inequality, and it is forming fast. The workers whose jobs already involved sitting at a computer are being pulled deeper into the AI economy. The workers whose jobs involved anything else are being left where they were.

What I cannot claim, and why it still matters
Honesty requires a caveat. This data observes Claude specifically, not all AI. Employer-deployed systems like scheduling software and corporate chatbots are invisible in consumer data.
But notice what that implies.
It means frontline workers encounter AI mostly as something embedded in employer systems and pointed at them, rather than as a tool in their own hands. Their schedules are optimized by AI they never open. Their scripts are written by AI they never consult. Their performance is scored by AI they never see.
Having run a frontline business, I do not think the gap is mysterious. No chatbot stocks a shelf or de-escalates an angry customer at closing. But frontline jobs are full of information work too. Inventory decisions. Vendor disputes. Scheduling puzzles. Customer complaint patterns worth spotting before they become viral.
My own experience says the real constraint is access. Shared devices. No uninterrupted time. The friction of opening an interface during a live operational problem. Meanwhile, supervisors and back-office staff can adopt the same tools freely.
The gap I measured is not physics. It is a stack of defaults. Product design that assumes a keyboard and a quiet room. Workplace policy that treats phone use as a discipline problem. Assumptions about who counts as the user when a new tool gets built.
Defaults can be changed by the people who set them.
A third of the workforce is currently participating in the AI economy mostly as its object, meaning the thing being scheduled and scripted, rather than as its users.
Whether that stays true is one of the most consequential open questions about this technology. I do not think it gets answered by better models. I think it gets answered by whoever decides what the next generation of workplace AI is actually for, and who it is actually built for.

The full working paper, replication code, and all data sources are at [github.com/raamnandhakumar-eng/polecoai]. email:raam.nandhakumar@gmail.com
